fact: December 20 is just ONE day away from the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere - the shortest day of the year when the sun barely peeks over the horizon!
In Antarctica right now, the sun never sets! Penguins are living in 24-hour daylight while kids in Alaska get only about 4 hours of sunshine.
Tree sap in cold regions has turned into nature's antifreeze by this date, preventing maple and birch trees from exploding when temperatures drop below freezing.
Squirrels have been frantically burying nuts for months, and by December 20, they've forgotten where they hid about 75% of their winter stash!
The moon looks extra bright and crisp around this time because cold winter air holds less water vapor, making the night sky crystal clear.
December 20 marks the peak time when bears in hibernation have slowed their heart rate down to just 8 beats per minute - that's slower than a sleeping human!
In the Southern Hemisphere, December 20 brings the opposite magic - it's almost summer solstice, so kids in Australia are getting ready for their longest day of the year!
Snow crystals forming around this date create the most perfect six-sided shapes because the air is cold enough for water to freeze instantly into geometric masterpieces.
December 20 falls during the time when reindeer and caribou have grown special winter nose hairs that warm up freezing air before it hits their lungs.
The sun sets so early in northern cities that by December 20, kids in places like Norway might not see daylight during their entire school day.
Evergreen trees are showing off right now because they're the only plants still green and photosynthesizing while everything else has gone dormant for winter.
